_metadata.chapter_title: Internal Morphology of the Spinal Cord and Brain in Stained Sections
_metadata.notes: Six images extracted: figures 6-23A through 6-26A are cross-sectional stained sections of midbrain levels; figure 6-27 is a vascular territory diagram; figure 6-28 is a sagittal/overview context image. Structures identified from label lines in each image.

anatomical_levels.description: Transverse section through the midbrain at the level of the inferior colliculus. The section passes through the caudal midbrain, showing the inferior colliculus dorsally, the cerebral peduncles ventrally, the red nucleus, substantia nigra, and the decussation of the superior cerebellar peduncle. The CN3 nucleus is present and the EW nucleus begins to appear. The MLF is prominent paramedian dorsally. Section is slightly caudal, corresponding to a T1-weighted MRI appearance at the inferior colliculus level.

tracts.notes: Large paired nuclear mass forming the dorsal tectum at this level; prominent bilateral rounded prominences
Visible as white fiber bundle running laterally from inferior colliculus toward medial geniculate nucleus; labeled on lateral aspect

tracts.zone: paramedian dorsal tegmentum, floor of cerebral aqueduct
tracts.notes: Paired paramedian bundles running longitudinally; critical for conjugate eye movements; INO when damaged
Surrounds the cerebral aqueduct; involved in descending pain modulation and autonomic control
Central CSF channel through midbrain; obstruction causes non-communicating hydrocephalus

tracts.somatotopy: somatotopic subgroups (SR, IR, MR, IO, levator palpebrae)
tracts.notes: Paired nucleus ventral to PAG at superior colliculus / inferior colliculus junction; fascicles course ventrally through red nucleus and tegmentum

tracts.zone: paramedian, caudal periaqueductal gray (at inferior colliculus level)
tracts.notes: Most caudal extraocular motor nucleus; fascicles decussate in the anterior medullary velum and exit dorsally — the only CN to exit dorsally

tracts.zone: lateral tegmentum, medial to spinothalamic tract
tracts.somatotopy: leg lateral, arm medial at this level
tracts.notes: At midbrain level, has rotated to occupy lateral tegmentum; carries fine touch, proprioception, vibration from contralateral body

tracts.zone: lateral tegmentum, lateral to medial lemniscus
tracts.notes: Lies lateral and slightly anterior to medial lemniscus in midbrain tegmentum; carries pain, temperature, crude touch
Large paired round nucleus in tegmentum; receives dentate (via SCP decussation) and corticorubral input; gives rise to rubrospinal tract; prominent at inferior colliculus level
Divided into pars compacta (dopaminergic neurons, dorsally) and pars reticulata (GABAergic, ventrally); degenerates in Parkinson disease
Contains melanin-pigmented dopaminergic neurons; lost in Parkinson disease
GABAergic output to thalamus (VA/VL) and superior colliculus; functionally analogous to globus pallidus internus

tracts.somatotopy: lateral 1/3 = corticospinal (leg lateral, arm medial); middle 3/5 = corticobulbar + corticospinal; medial 1/5 = frontopontine fibers
tracts.notes: Massive white matter bundle ventral to substantia nigra; contains corticospinal (lateral), corticobulbar (middle), frontopontine (medial) and parietopontine/occipitopontine (lateral) fibers; CN3 fascicles exit between peduncle and SN
Occupies middle 3/5 of cerebral peduncle; will continue into basis pontis and then pyramids

tracts.zone: middle cerebral peduncle, medial to corticospinal
tracts.somatotopy: none (bilateral cortical control of most CN motor nuclei)
tracts.notes: Travels with corticospinal in peduncle; provides cortical input to contralateral (and some bilateral) cranial nerve motor nuclei
From frontal cortex to ipsilateral pontine nuclei; part of corticopontocerebellar pathway
From temporal/parietal cortex to ipsilateral pontine nuclei

tracts.tract_name: Superior cerebellar peduncle (decussation of SCP / brachium conjunctivum)
tracts.zone: ventral tegmentum at inferior colliculus level
tracts.somatotopy: somatotopic (body parts organized within dentate nucleus axons)
tracts.clinical_pathway: cerebellar outflow (dentate to red nucleus / VL thalamus)
tracts.notes: Decussation occurs in caudal midbrain tegmentum ventral to red nucleus; damage causes contralateral cerebellar ataxia (crossed effect)
Auditory pathway from cochlear nuclei/SOC ascending to inferior colliculus; visible in lateral tegmentum at this level
Unique: pseudounipolar cell bodies within CNS; carries proprioception from jaw muscles; runs in lateral PAG throughout midbrain
Major serotonergic nucleus; projects widely to forebrain; involved in mood, arousal
Ascending reticular activating system (ARAS); lesions cause coma; includes midbrain reticular nucleus

tracts.zone: ventral tegmentum, coursing through red nucleus to exit medial to cerebral peduncle
tracts.notes: Exit medially between cerebral peduncle and pons (interpeduncular fossa); vulnerable to PCA/PCom aneurysm causing CN3 palsy with pupil dilation
Receives input from habenula via fasciculus retroflexus; involved in limbic and reward circuits

tracts.zone: ventral tegmentum midline, below red nucleus
tracts.notes: Rubrospinal fibers decussate immediately ventral to red nucleus (Forel's decussation); then descend contralaterally

anatomical_levels.level_name: Inferior Colliculus Level (slightly rostral variant) / CN3 fascicle level
anatomical_levels.description: Transverse section through the midbrain at a level slightly rostral to or through the caudal inferior colliculus, showing prominent Purkinje cell/dentate outflow (SCP decussation still visible or just completed), red nucleus enlarging, and CN3 fascicles clearly visible traversing the tegmentum toward the interpeduncular fossa. The periaqueductal gray, MLF, and medial lemniscus are well-defined. The substantia nigra and cerebral peduncles are large. Corticospinal and corticopontine somatotopy is visible in the peduncle.

tracts.notes: At this level the inferior colliculus nuclei are still visible bilaterally in the tectum
Connects inferior colliculus to medial geniculate nucleus; visible as lateral surface bundle
Continuous longitudinal column; stimulation produces analgesia
Well-defined paired bundles; INO caused by lesion here (usually demyelination in MS)
Larger at this level; receives contralateral dentate nucleus input (via SCP); gives rise to rubrospinal tract after decussation

tracts.zone: ventral tegmentum, traversing between CN3 nucleus and interpeduncular fossa
tracts.notes: Fascicles pass through medial red nucleus and then through substantia nigra to exit in interpeduncular fossa; Benedict syndrome if red nucleus damaged with CN3 fascicles

tracts.zone: dorsal to CN3 nucleus, in midline PAG
tracts.notes: Preganglionic parasympathetic neurons; fibers travel with CN3 on its dorsomedial surface; pupillary light reflex efferent limb; first affected in transtentorial herniation (fixed dilated pupil)

tracts.zone: ventral midbrain between tegmentum and peduncle
tracts.notes: Continuous from inferior to superior colliculus level; divided into compact (dorsal) and reticular (ventral) zones

tracts.somatotopy: medial = frontopontine; middle 3/5 = corticospinal/corticobulbar; lateral = temporopontine/parietopontine
tracts.notes: Same somatotopic organization as MB-01; fibers converge from internal capsule
Has rotated from medial position (medulla) to lateral position (midbrain); adjacent to spinothalamic tract

tracts.zone: lateral tegmentum, anterolateral to medial lemniscus
tracts.notes: In close proximity to medial lemniscus at midbrain level; converge toward thalamus (VPL)
At this level decussation is completing; fibers projecting to contralateral red nucleus and thalamus
Visible at this level as midline cell group

tracts.tract_name: Mesencephalic nucleus and tract of CN5
tracts.notes: Continues throughout midbrain in lateral PAG
ARAS; surrounds and lies between named nuclei

tracts.zone: dorsal midline, between bilateral inferior colliculi
tracts.notes: Crosses midline connecting bilateral inferior colliculi; important for binaural hearing and sound localization

anatomical_levels.level_name: Superior Colliculus Level (with EW nucleus and CN3)
anatomical_levels.description: Transverse section through the rostral midbrain at the level of the superior colliculus. This is the classic 'oculomotor level' showing the superior colliculus in the tectum, the prominent oculomotor (CN3) nucleus and Edinger-Westphal nucleus in the periaqueductal gray, large red nucleus, substantia nigra, and cerebral peduncles. The MLF, medial lemniscus, and spinothalamic tract maintain their established positions. The SCP decussation has passed. This level corresponds to the 'CN3 exit level' in clinical teaching.

tracts.notes: Multilayered structure; superficial layers receive retinal and cortical visual input; deep layers coordinate eye and head movements; involved in pupillary light reflex (pretectal relay)

tracts.tract_name: Pretectal area (rostral to SC, visible at this level)
tracts.notes: Olivary pretectal nucleus relays pupillary light reflex to bilateral EW nuclei (via posterior commissure)
Carries fibers crossing to contralateral EW nucleus for consensual pupillary light reflex; lesion here causes Parinaud syndrome (upgaze palsy)

tracts.somatotopy: SR, IR, MR, IO, levator palpebrae subgroups
tracts.notes: Most prominent at superior colliculus level; controls all extraocular muscles except SO (CN4) and LR (CN6)

tracts.system: parasympathetic — pupil constriction and lens accommodation
tracts.notes: Preganglionic parasympathetic; fibers travel in superficial dorsomedial CN3; compressed first in uncal herniation causing fixed dilated pupil
Still prominent at this level; connects CN3 nucleus caudally to CN6 nucleus in pons; lesion = INO
Fully developed at this level; damage produces contralateral intention tremor (dentato-rubral pathway disruption) or ipsilateral CN3 palsy with contralateral cerebellar signs (Benedict syndrome)
Pass through medial red nucleus then between peduncle and SN to exit in interpeduncular cistern; vulnerable to posterior communicating artery aneurysm
Continuous structure; pars compacta and reticulata still distinguishable

tracts.somatotopy: same as lower levels: frontopontine medial, corticospinal/corticobulbar middle, temporopontine lateral
tracts.notes: Weber syndrome: ipsilateral CN3 palsy + contralateral hemiplegia from peduncle + CN3 fascicle infarct
Ascending to VPL thalamus; in lateral tegmentum at midbrain level

tracts.zone: lateral tegmentum, lateral/anterior to medial lemniscus
tracts.notes: Merging with medial lemniscus as they jointly ascend to thalamus
Carries nociceptive information to superior colliculus for orienting responses to pain

tracts.tract_name: Lateral lemniscus (terminating in inferior colliculus above)
tracts.notes: Largely terminated in inferior colliculus at lower level; residual fibers still visible here
Still present throughout midbrain PAG

tracts.tract_name: Reticular formation (midbrain central gray / cuneiform nucleus)
tracts.notes: ARAS; cuneiform and sub-cuneiform nuclei in lateral reticular formation at this level
After crossing in Forel's decussation (lower level), descends in lateral tegmentum; prominent in non-human primates, relatively small in humans
Contains rubro-olivary fibers descending; palatal myoclonus when damaged (inferior olivary hypertrophy)

anatomical_levels.level_name: Pretectal / Rostral Midbrain — Diencephalon Junction Level
anatomical_levels.description: Transverse section through the transition zone between the rostral midbrain and the caudal diencephalon (subthalamus / posterior thalamus). The superior colliculus or pretectal area is visible dorsally. The pulvinar and posterior thalamic nuclei begin to appear. The cerebral peduncles are still visible but narrowing. The MLF continues. This level is important for understanding the pretectal pupillary reflex circuit and Parinaud syndrome. The posterior commissure and pineal gland region are visible.

tracts.notes: Receives retinal ganglion cell input via optic tract; projects bilaterally (via posterior commissure) to EW nuclei; lesion disrupts afferent limb of PLR
Uppermost part of SC visible at this transition level

Connects pretectal nuclei bilaterally; also carries posterior commissure fibers from habenular commissure region; Parinaud syndrome caused by lesions compressing posterior commissure/pretectum (e.g., pineal tumors)
Largest thalamic nucleus; multisensory; receives input from superior colliculus and visual cortex; involved in visual attention
Auditory thalamic relay; visible as prominent lateral prominence at this level; brachium of IC terminates here

tracts.vascular_territory: PCA (anterior choroidal artery for anterior portion)
tracts.notes: Primary visual thalamic relay; receives optic tract input; projects to V1 via optic radiation

tracts.vascular_territory: Posterior thalamoperforators / subthalamic branches of PCA
tracts.notes: Lesion causes hemiballismus (contralateral wild flinging movements); target for DBS in Parkinson disease
Receives widespread input; modulates thalamic activity; DBS target for essential tremor
H2 = lenticular fasciculus (GPi output); H1 = thalamic fasciculus (to VL thalamus); H field = prerubral field (convergence of pallidothalamic and cerebellothalamic fibers)
Continues rostrally; at this level becoming less distinct as it merges with subthalamic/midbrain reticular fiber systems

tracts.tract_name: Cerebral peduncle (upper portion / internal capsule crus)
tracts.zone: ventral, transitioning to posterior limb internal capsule
tracts.notes: At this level the peduncle merges into the posterior limb of internal capsule
Descends from habenular nucleus (epithalamus) to interpeduncular nucleus; involved in reward and aversion circuitry
Approaching VPL thalamus; still in lateral tegmentum at this level

tracts.zone: lateral tegmentum, adjacent to medial lemniscus
tracts.notes: Both medial lemniscus and STT now closely approximated as they enter VPL thalamus
ARAS continues into subthalamus; Nauta's midline thalamic nuclei are part of this system
Pineal tumors cause Parinaud syndrome by compressing posterior commissure/pretectal area; visible at this level

anatomical_levels.level_name: Vascular Territory Diagram — Midbrain (Inferior and Superior Colliculus Levels)
anatomical_levels.description: This image (figure 6-27) is a dedicated vascular territory diagram showing two midbrain cross-sections with arterial supply territories demarcated. The upper section corresponds to the inferior colliculus level; the lower section to the superior colliculus / rostral midbrain level. Color-coded zones show territories of: (1) anteromedial basilar perforators, (2) anterolateral basilar perforators / short circumferential arteries, (3) lateral PCA / long circumferential arteries (quadrigeminal artery), (4) posterior/dorsal PCA territory. Clinical vascular syndromes are named (Weber, Claude, Benedict, Parinaud). The image also labels key structures in relation to arterial territories.

tracts.clinical_pathway: motor-corticospinal tract + motor-Corticobulbar tract + CN3/CN4 related + motor-Rubrospinal
tracts.notes: Supplies: cerebral peduncle (medial), substantia nigra (medial), CN3 fascicles, red nucleus, MLF, interpeduncular nucleus. Infarct here: Weber syndrome (peduncle + CN3) or Benedict syndrome (red nucleus + CN3)

tracts.vascular_territory: Short circumferential branches of basilar / SCA
tracts.notes: Supplies: cerebral peduncle (lateral), substantia nigra (lateral). Isolated peduncle or SN infarcts

tracts.clinical_pathway: sensory-Medial lemniscus + spinothalamic tract + auditory relay
tracts.notes: Supplies: lateral tegmentum (medial lemniscus, spinothalamic tract, SCP), brachium of inferior/superior colliculus, lateral lemniscus

tracts.clinical_pathway: auditory relay + visual reflex + EW nucleus territory
tracts.vascular_territory: PCA (posterior / tectal branches — quadrigeminal plate arteries)
tracts.notes: Supplies: inferior colliculus, superior colliculus, pretectal area, PAG, inferior/superior brachium. Damage: contralateral hearing loss (IC), Parinaud syndrome (pretectal), aqueductal obstruction
Weber syndrome: ipsilateral CN3 palsy (complete: ptosis, mydriasis, eye down-and-out) + contralateral hemiplegia from cerebral peduncle infarct

tracts.color_in_atlas: labeled arrow to red nucleus + CN3 zone
tracts.vascular_territory: PCA (paramedian mesencephalic, slightly deeper than Weber)
tracts.notes: Benedict syndrome: ipsilateral CN3 palsy + contralateral tremor/ataxia (red nucleus or dentato-rubral pathway). No hemiplegia (peduncle spared)
Claude syndrome: ipsilateral CN3 palsy + contralateral cerebellar ataxia (SCP/dentato-thalamic pathway affected). Combination of Weber + Benedict features sometimes described

tracts.clinical_pathway: EW nucleus + pupillary light reflex arc
tracts.notes: Parinaud (dorsal midbrain) syndrome: upgaze palsy, convergence-retraction nystagmus, light-near dissociation, lid retraction (Collier sign). Caused by pineal tumors, hydrocephalus, or dorsal PCA infarct

anatomical_levels.level_name: Overview / Sagittal Context — Brainstem with MRI Correlation
anatomical_levels.description: This image (figure 6-28) provides a sagittal or oblique overview/context diagram showing the entire brainstem from medulla to diencephalon, with labeled major structures in their longitudinal relationships. It serves as an orientation image for the cross-sectional atlas plates and shows the MRI correlate. Key structures are labeled in their rostro-caudal positions. The image shows the relationship between the midbrain levels (inferior colliculus, superior colliculus, pretectal), pons, medulla oblongata, and transition to the diencephalon. Additional structures visible include the cerebellum, fourth ventricle, and major fiber tracts in sagittal view.

tracts.notes: Confirms rostro-caudal extent of midbrain from pontomedullary junction to midbrain-diencephalon junction (posterior commissure)

tracts.somatotopy: somatotopic (genu = frontal, body = parietal, splenium = occipital/temporal)
tracts.notes: Visible in sagittal context to orient the section level

tracts.zone: subcallosal, arching from hippocampus to mammillary body
tracts.notes: Major hippocampal output; damage causes anterograde amnesia

tracts.clinical_pathway: sensory-Medial lemniscus + spinothalamic tract + cerebellar outflow
tracts.notes: Sagittal view shows thalamic shape and relationship to midbrain
Descending sympathetic fibers from hypothalamus pass through dorsolateral tegmentum of brainstem

tracts.somatotopy: somatotopic (anterior lobe = spinal input; posterior lobe = cortical input)
tracts.notes: Connected to brainstem via 3 peduncles; visible in sagittal context
Connects aqueduct superiorly to central canal / cisterna magna inferiorly via foramina of Luschka and Magendie
Sagittal context; contains CN5, CN6, CN7, CN8 nuclei; corticospinal fibers scattered in basis pontis

tracts.vascular_territory: Vertebral artery + PICA + anterior spinal artery
tracts.notes: Sagittal context; pyramidal decussation and sensory decussation (internal arcuate fibers) occur here

tracts.tract_name: Lateral longitudinal stria / stria medullaris thalami
tracts.notes: Connects septal nuclei, amygdala, and other limbic areas to habenular nucleus; runs on dorsomedial thalamic surface

tracts.zone: vertical tract in lateral hypothalamus / anterior thalamus
tracts.notes: From mammillary body to anterior thalamic nucleus; key Papez circuit component; damage causes anterograde amnesia (Wernicke-Korsakoff)
Courses from dentate nucleus rostrally through lateral pontine/midbrain tegmentum to decussate in caudal midbrain; sagittal view shows full trajectory
Main cerebellar afferent from spinal cord and medullary nuclei; PICA occlusion (Wallenberg) damages ICP causing ipsilateral ataxia
Largest cerebellar peduncle; carries pontine nuclei output to contralateral cerebellar hemisphere; damaged in lateral pontine infarcts

corticospinal_tract.somatotopy: Leg most lateral, arm medial, face most medial within corticospinal zone
corticospinal_tract.clinical_syndrome: Weber syndrome (with CN3), pure motor hemiplegia if isolated peduncle

corticobulbar_tract.midbrain_location: Middle cerebral peduncle, medial to corticospinal fibers

rubrospinal_tract.midbrain_location: Decussates ventral to red nucleus (Forel's decussation), then lateral tegmentum
rubrospinal_tract.clinical_syndrome: Benedict syndrome (with CN3 and red nucleus)

medial_lemniscus.midbrain_location: Lateral tegmentum at all midbrain levels (rotated from medial medullary position)
medial_lemniscus.clinical_syndrome: Contralateral loss of proprioception, vibration, fine touch

spinothalamic_tract.midbrain_location: Lateral tegmentum, adjacent and lateral to medial lemniscus
spinothalamic_tract.clinical_syndrome: Contralateral loss of pain and temperature

CN3_nucleus.midbrain_location: Paramedian ventral PAG, inferior and superior colliculus levels
CN3_nucleus.clinical_syndrome: Complete CN3 palsy (ptosis, mydriasis, eye down-and-out)

EW_nucleus.midbrain_location: Rostral to CN3 nucleus, midline PAG (superior colliculus level)
EW_nucleus.clinical_syndrome: Mydriasis (dilated fixed pupil); first sign in uncal herniation

CN4_nucleus.midbrain_location: Caudal PAG at inferior colliculus level; only CN to exit dorsally after decussation in anterior medullary velum
CN4_nucleus.clinical_syndrome: CN4 palsy: vertical diplopia, head tilt away from lesion, hypertropia

MLF.clinical_syndrome: INO (internuclear ophthalmoplegia): ipsilateral adduction failure on lateral gaze, contralateral abducting nystagmus

vascular_syndromes.deficits: Ipsilateral CN3 palsy + contralateral tremor/ataxia (no hemiplegia)
Ipsilateral CN3 palsy + contralateral cerebellar ataxia
Upgaze palsy, convergence-retraction nystagmus, light-near dissociation, Collier sign (lid retraction)
Ipsilateral CN3 palsy + ipsilateral cerebellar ataxia (rare)
